app.title: Inequalculator
app.tagline: How asset returns above the growth of the economy shape wealth over time
app.intro: The economy grows for everyone — but assets tend to compound faster than wages. Explore who ends up owning the growth.
app.rgLine: This is the mechanism economists call r > g: when the return on assets (r) outpaces the growth of the overall economy (g), wealth concentrates.

country.label: Country
country.help: Load real-world starting figures for each country. Every number shows its source. Editing a core slider switches to Custom.

controls.assetReturnHelp: The real, inflation-adjusted return the top group earns on its assets each year. Higher-wealth groups earn a share of this; the bottom 50% grows with the economy. Historically, broad stock markets have returned roughly 5–7% real.
controls.economyGrowthHelp: The real growth rate of the wider economy each year — the return applied to the bottom 50%. This moves the tail end of the lines up or down.
controls.yearHelp: Scrub through time, or press play to watch wealth compound from year 0 to the horizon.
controls.playAria: Play the animation from year 0 to the end
controls.resetAria: Reset all values to the selected country preset

readout.start: At the start, the top 1% owns {{top1}} and the top 10% owns {{top10}} of all wealth.
readout.main: Everyone is {{multiple}} richer than year 0 — but the top 1% now owns {{top1}} and the top 10% owns {{top10}}.
readout.note: Both groups are richer in absolute terms. What changes is who owns the growth.

pie.title: Who owns the wealth
pie.caption: Economy {{multiple}} larger than year 0
pie.a11y: Donut chart. At year {{year}}, total wealth is {{multiple}} its year-0 size. The top 1% owns {{top1}}, the next 9% {{next9}}, the middle 40% {{middle40}}, and the bottom 50% {{bottom50}}.

divergence.title: How the shares diverge over time
divergence.a11y: Stacked area chart of each group's share of total wealth from year 0 to year {{years}}. At the selected year {{year}}, the top 1% holds {{top1}} and the top 10% holds {{top10}}.
divergence.scrubHint: Tap or drag on the chart to change the year

tax.title: Wealth tax
tax.subtitle: Choose a tax design and watch it bend the curve.
tax.redistributeHelp: Where the collected tax goes each year. It is a transfer within the private economy unless set to “not redistributed”.
tax.thresholdNote: Threshold taxes are modeled within the top 10% — most accurate for the top 1%.
tax.caveatLink: Read the caveats on wealth taxes

flatTop10.name: Flat 1% on the top 10%
flatTop10.desc: A flat annual rate on the entire wealth of the top decile.

threshold10m.name: 2% above 10M
threshold10m.desc: A marginal 2%/yr on the wealth each household holds above 10M.

warren.name: Warren 2% / 3%
warren.desc: 2% above 50M and 3% above 1B — the US Ultra-Millionaire Tax.

zucman.name: Zucman 2% above 100M
zucman.desc: A 2% minimum on fortunes above 100M — the G20 blueprint.

spain.name: Spain-style progressive
spain.desc: Rising bands from ~0.2% to 3.5%, like Spain's wealth + solidarity tax.

swiss.name: Swiss-style broad
swiss.desc: A low 0.6%/yr on net wealth above 100K, like Swiss cantons.

advanced.title: Advanced settings
advanced.subtitle: More factors, for exploring the mechanism in detail.
advanced.startingHelp: Each group's share of total wealth at year 0. The middle 40% is the remainder.
advanced.timeHorizonHelp: How many years to project forward.
advanced.returnsNote: Assumption: higher-wealth groups earn higher real returns — the top 1% earns the asset return r, the bottom 50% the economy rate g, the others in between (Fagereng et al. 2020).

country.title: Pick a country
country.body: Load real starting figures for the US, UK or Germany — or build your own with Custom.

rates.title: Move the two rates
rates.body: Asset return (r) is what the top earns; economy growth (g) is the bottom 50%. When r beats g, wealth concentrates.

year.title: Travel through time
year.body: Drag the year, or press play, to watch the effect compound over decades.

info.title: Sources & assumptions
info.modelBody: Inequalculator is a stylized compounding model. It splits the population into four groups — the top 1%, the next 9%, the middle 40% and the bottom 50% — and compounds each group's wealth forward one year at a time using real, inflation-adjusted returns. It is an illustration of one mechanism, not a forecast of any country's future.
info.rgBody: If the money you already own grows faster than the wages and output of the economy as a whole, then — all else equal — the people who own the most assets capture a growing share of total wealth over time. Thomas Piketty summarized this as r > g: the return on capital (r) exceeding the growth rate of the economy (g).
info.returnsHeading: Why the groups earn different returns
info.returnsBody: Wealthier households have historically earned higher real returns (more equities and business equity, less cash, cheaper leverage). The model reflects this with a gradient: the bottom 50% grows at the economy rate g, the top 1% at the asset rate r, and the middle groups in between. This is what makes the very top pull away from the merely rich.
info.paretoBody: A tax like “2% on wealth above 10M” depends on actual wealth levels, so the model is anchored to each country's total private wealth and household count, and the top 10% is modeled as a Pareto distribution whose shape is calibrated from the top-1%-vs-top-10% concentration. Threshold and progressive taxes are computed in closed form over that tail. It is most accurate for the top 1%, where real wealth taxes bite.
info.realReturnsNote: All returns in this tool are real (inflation-adjusted), so the growth you see is on top of inflation.

leavesOut.twoGroup: Within each group everyone is treated as equal (except the Pareto tail used for taxes), which understates inequality inside the top.
leavesOut.mobility: People move between groups over their lives, and new fortunes enter the top, which fixed groups do not capture.
leavesOut.empirical: Empirically, top wealth shares have risen sharply in the United States but have been comparatively flatter in the UK and Germany.
leavesOut.housing: A prominent academic view (Matthew Rognlie) attributes much of the rising capital share specifically to housing.
leavesOut.wealthTax: The effectiveness of a wealth tax is debated: valuation is hard, avoidance and capital flight are real, and the model applies no avoidance, so its revenue is an upper bound. Germany's own wealth tax was suspended in 1997 over unequal asset valuation.

info.twoSidedNote: You can test all of this yourself by moving r, g, the group shares and the tax lever, and comparing countries.
info.privacyBody: This tool runs entirely in your browser. It collects no personal data, sets no cookies, and makes no network requests. Only your theme choice and a first-visit flag are stored locally on your device.
info.disclaimer: An illustration of one mechanism, not a forecast or financial advice.

footer.disclaimer: A stylized model using real (inflation-adjusted) returns. An illustration of one mechanism — not a forecast or financial advice.
footer.builtWith: All figures are illustrative anchors; each shows its source.

error.title: Something went wrong
error.body: The calculator hit an unexpected state. Reloading should fix it.
